transcript.text: Okay, so I remember another aborigine town that I stopped in, it was the same deal, I had to find where they lived and get them to open up the store and there were some other aborigines who wanted to buy stuff and I guess the aborigines couldn't even communicate with each other because each aboriginal tribe has their own language, but maybe supposedly they could telepathically communicate with each other.

But, yeah, at some points in this road though, it got kind of, it was really wild, like it wasn't a wide road, it was just two tracks on sand that I was riding down and I had to push my bike for a lot of it and then I'd ride for some of it.
And so, yeah, it was usually like 200 miles between the places and every three miles, three hours, a car would drive by. One guy gave me some water on a car, a tour guide.
Yeah, some of the cars that drove by were actually tourists and I think I saw some people in mountain bikes with a support vehicle who were maybe doing the crossing.
And then I finally made it to where it was just a dirt road and some guy offered to drive me 50 miles into some other town.
And then I rode into, it got paved and I rode into Calgary Kyle is the name of the town.
And that was kind of, that was interesting. It was like a wild west type of a town.
Mining town.
And I stayed in a hostel and a lot of people in my hostel were Australians who were miners.
And I went into the, uh, um, I went into a bar and it was like the wild west.
All the waitresses were dressed in G strings and, um, and that's funny.

And, uh, and then I rode into Perth and it was interesting writing South because every day I could feel it getting colder and colder until I had to start sleeping in my tent to stay warm because I didn't have a warm sleeping bag.
And, um, then I started having to sleep on the side of the road like it was more populated and kind of, I guess, coming off the road a little bit.

Uh, but yeah, talking to some of the Australians, like white Australians where I would buy stuff on the side of the road, they were kind of like worried about me thinking I'd get attacked by us, by aborigines.
They thought they were dangerous.
They're like, you know, I hope you hide from those aborigines and they didn't seem dangerous to me.
They seem cool.
But I remember at one point I was camping out outside of a town.
It was actually a town I was outside of, but I thought I was way out in the middle of nowhere.
And I was just sitting there and this aborigine guy walks up to me and he's, and he's, I was like, wow, what are you doing?
He's just, oh, I'm just on my walkabout.
I just live right up there.
And, um, so I said, oh, wow, I didn't know that, you know, there were any houses around here.
And, uh, I asked him, I was asking him about aborigines, like how they lived.
And I said, where, like, how do you eat?
What do you eat?
And they hunted, um, they hunted deer.
And I remember hearing that, like, Native Americans would actually run down deer.
And then, like, they'd run, you know, humans can run for 24 hours straight.
They're the only animals that can do that.
And so they could actually run down deer and then they get tired and then they spear them.
So I said, well, what do you do?
Like, run after the, uh, kangaroos and spear them?
And he's, like, he looked at me like I was stupid.
He said, what?
No way.
That's, that would be stupid.
No, you just go to the watering hole and you wait there until it comes to drink.
And then you throw your spear at it.
And, um, uh, I actually never saw any kangaroos.
I never saw one live kangaroo the whole time I rode across Australia ice.
I saw, I saw a dead kangaroo like every maybe minute.
So I basically smelled the smell of dead flesh about half the time I was riding.
I was smelling dead flesh.
I eventually started to enjoy it.
I got used to it and started to enjoy the smell of dead flesh.
There were just, I saw thousands and thousands of dead kangaroos on the side of the road.
And at one point I saw, like, six wallabies that were alive.
But that's all I saw.
Um, I guess what happens is the kangaroos that come to the side of the road and because of the ditch that people make on the side of the road to help drain or something,
The grass goes there so they drink it and then the cars drive by and they get hypnotized by the light and jump in the middle of the road and get killed.
And every single car has cow catchers.
It has those big, huge metal bumpers on them.
Um, and I saw one dead emu.
And at one point I saw a live emu that actually ran alongside me for ten minutes.
Um, and, um, so, um, yeah.
So I knew I made, I made it to Calgary and then I rode down south into Perth and it was the first time there were actually trees.
And it was also the first time that I actually saw hills or like little bits of hills and I actually went down a little bit of a descent while I went into Perth.
Um, and stayed in Perth for a couple of days, which is one of the most beautiful cities I've ever seen because they have this, uh, bay that comes in forever.

And an American kid who was riding across the, who actually rode across the desert that I did in the other direction told me that there's some island out there off the coast that's all bike paths that I was going to go out and do, but I didn't go around to it.
Um, and I saw the premiere of the first, the first remake of, you know, Star Wars Return of the Phantom Menace or something, um, in Perth.
Everybody dressed up in Star Wars uniforms to see that.
Um, I was surprised that Perth, there was so many Asians though.
It seemed like half the people in Perth were Asian.
Um, and they wouldn't let me read their magazines in their, in their shops.
Um, but I didn't buy any.
Um, and it was nice to eat a more, eat something other than hamburgers.
The whole time I rode across Australia from Darwin to Perth, I was, I ate hamburgers.
I'd eat, I had to buy like seven hamburgers and I'd just wrap them up and throw them in my bag and eat and chow on them.
Uh, and they were kind of expensive.
It wasn't cheap to ride across Australia because I had a big appetite and those hamburgers aren't, aren't cheap, but they were good.
They put beets on them.
We don't put beets in hamburgers in America.
I guess they call them beets something.
I can't remember what the full name of it.
But, uh, so I was in Perth for a couple of days and I rode down to the coast, almost to the coast.
At one point on one day.
And, uh, it was the first time I was in a, like a first world big city since I was in Europe.
And then I rode north along the edge of Australia.
And, uh, on one day I decided to see if I could do an endurance ride because I was getting strong, stronger, better shape.
And I rode for 30 hours straight and it was 500 kilometers, about 300 miles.
Um, I'd stop every three hours for like a half hour smoke break.
By then I was getting really, I was building up a tolerance to the marijuana.
So I'd have to smoke for like a half hour to get high.
And in order to keep my blood sugars up, I would buy a big bag of jelly beans and I'd basically eat the entire bag of jelly beans while I'm eating, smoking my weed.
And so, yeah, I rode through.
But I was riding pretty strong and hard.
And I had, I was, I was riding with normal hiking shoes and straps, toe clips.
So my feet were like an agonizing pain for riding that long.
And I remember I stopped after 30 hours.
I stopped at like five o'clock in the afternoon.
And it was, uh, I, I felt great.
My joints were aching a little bit, but I was totally alert, totally awake.
My legs were totally fresh.
I felt like I could have gone another 24 hours.
And I probably should have tried it, but I stopped just because out of boredom.
And so I stopped and I sat down and I was so riled up I couldn't even get to sleep.
I just got stoned and just sat there like and laid down and probably should have kept riding.
So I thought to myself, man, I'm going to go for a 48 hour ride.
So I got on my bike and I was all excited about a 48 hour ride, but then my, my rim cracked in half.
So I had to get on a bus and take the bus in the next town, which was Broome.
Um, and I stayed in Broome for four days while I waited for another wheel, which I ordered, but I ordered for another place.
It was actually a, like a, a, a, a tri spoke or a four spoke plastic wheel, really heavy, like a cheap one.
Um, and I got that and, uh, rode north, but for some, and then I tried to do that ride again, but then my, uh, two day ride again, but then my chain, I forgot to find what wouldn't matter.
The chain that I was writing stretched out and I couldn't ride even five miles an hour without it totally slipping.
So I stopped at a truck stop, or I guess they had these, uh, crocodiles that you could look at some river.
I never went down to look at them, but, um, uh, this Dutch guy, really old Dutch guy who actually grew up in Indonesia and was part of Dutch.
And he was in a concentration camp for a while there, told me all the story, but he drove me up to Darwin where I bought a new chain and a new sprockets.
And, uh, I went to a fair and, uh, had some long religious conversation with some evangelists and, uh, bought some weed off of some guy who sold like watermelons.
And, uh, and then I rode down, down from Darwin again and rode pretty much halfway to the guy who drove me back to Darwin and his truck drove me maybe a hundred miles or something.
Well, it didn't drive me that far.
And then, and then this guy, I drove, I rode almost to, um, Issa, I think it's called, I think it's technically the Bigelips.
I have to stop.
